concerns.text: The plan performs a binomial test of the overall inconsistency proportion against a fixed baseline of 0.05, but provides no power analysis or justification for the chosen baseline proportion. Without estimating the required sample size (number of URLs) to detect a meaningful deviation from 0.05, the prevalence estimate may be under‑powered or misleading.

Selection bias is not addressed: the corpus of publicly available A/B test summaries may not be representative of all industry experiments, yet the plan treats the observed inconsistency rate as a general prevalence estimate. No strategy (e.g., stratified sampling, bias assessment) is described to mitigate this confound.

Extraction relies on heuristic XPath/regex patterns without a robust validation beyond a 30‑item validation set. A validation set of only 30 summaries is likely insufficient to reliably estimate extraction accuracy (≥ 95 %) and does not allow assessment of variability across different source formats, threatening construct validity of the extracted metrics.

The inconsistency thresholds (e.g., absolute p‑value difference > 0.05 or > max(0.01, 0.2 × reconstructed p)) are adopted from the specification but lack empirical justification. Arbitrary tolerances may inflate false‑positive inconsistency rates, biasing the prevalence estimate.
